description:
You are acting as a security auditor who has been asked to demonstrate a
    simple, permission-aware file-synchronisation procedure.

    1. Source tree  
       • Path: /home/user/project  
       • Already contains various files with mixed permissions (some are meant
         to stay private).  
       • You must copy ONLY those files whose “other” (world) read bit is set.  
         In octal notation this means any file whose permission mask ends in 4,
         5, 6 or 7 (e.g. 644, 664, 755 …).  
       • Sub-directories must be preserved.

    2. Destination tree  
       • Path: /home/user/simulated_remote  
       • Start with it empty.  
       • When you are done, it must contain an exact sub-directory layout and
         a copy of ONLY the world-readable files from the source.  
       • All copied files must retain their original permissions.

    3. Actions you have to perform  
       a. Synchronise the qualifying files (3–4 shell commands should suffice;  
          rsync is recommended but not enforced).  
       b. Produce a plain-text report file at  
          /home/user/sync_report.log.

    4. Mandatory report format (STRICTLY follow this so automated grading works)  
       • One file per line.  
       • Relative path from /home/user/project (no leading “./”).  
       • Exactly one space character.  
       • Three-digit octal permission mask (as reported by `stat -c %a`).  
       • Lines must be sorted in ASCII order.  
       • A final trailing newline character is required.

       Example (illustrative):  
       README.md 644\n  
       src/main.c 644\n

    5. Success criteria checked by the grader  
       • /home/user/simulated_remote contains only the expected files.  
       • Each copied file’s permission mask matches that in the source.  
       • /home/user/sync_report.log exists, is readable, and matches the exact
         format described above.  
       • Log file lines and destination tree contents match one another.

    6. Hints (do NOT reveal hidden data)  
       • Use find’s `-perm` or similar filter to detect world-readable files.  
       • `rsync -a --files-from` can copy only a given list.  
       • Remember to create the log file AFTER syncing so you can stat the
         resulting files for their permissions.  
       • Keep command count small (3–4) as requested.

    IMPORTANT: The grader matches file names, permissions and the report file
    byte-for-byte. Carefully follow the exact report specification.
truth:
Initial filesystem state BEFORE the agent begins:

    Directory: /home/user/project
        index.html         (permissions 0644, contents: "<html>Index</html>\n")
        secret.txt         (permissions 0600, contents: "top secret\n")
        readme.md          (permissions 0664, contents: "Project read-me\n")
        .env               (permissions 0600, contents: "DB_PASS=1234\n")
        Sub-directory: /home/user/project/public
            style.css      (permissions 0644, contents: "body {margin:0;}\n")
            script.sh      (permissions 0755, contents: "#!/bin/bash\necho hi\n")

    Directory: /home/user/simulated_remote
        (empty, permissions 0755)

    World-readable files that MUST be synchronised:
        index.html   644
        public/style.css  644
        public/script.sh  755
        readme.md    664

    Files that MUST NOT be synchronised:
        secret.txt
        .env

    Expected final file list inside /home/user/simulated_remote (with identical
    sub-paths and permissions):
        index.html         0644
        public/style.css   0644
        public/script.sh   0755
        readme.md          0664

    Expected exact contents of /home/user/sync_report.log (including trailing newline):
        index.html 644\n
        public/script.sh 755\n
        public/style.css 644\n
        readme.md 664\n
        (Lines sorted ASCII; note script.sh line comes before style.css.)

    The grader will:
        • Stat each file under simulated_remote and compare to the above list.
        • Read /home/user/sync_report.log and check byte-for-byte equality with
          the expected block shown above.
